step.title: Step 22
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='Make node 12 purple to enable SPACE')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 33, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 32 of about 55 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so SPACE merged node 13+14 into a white (100px) block. My model is confirmed:
- 3 blocks + node 12 ≠ node 13 → SPACE merges bottom two, result takes node 12's color
Current state: node 12=white (84px), node 13=white (100px)
Both are white. I need to figure out what the goal state is.
Let me think about this from scratch. The panel shows white on top, purple on bottom. The initial state had purple on top, white on bottom. The goal is to swap them.
To get white on top, purple on bottom:
- Node 12 needs to be white ✓
- Node 13 needs to be purple
But SPACE always makes the result take node 12's color. So if node 12=white, any merge produces white.
The only way to get a purple block in the main area is to:
1. Have node 12=purple
2. SPACE to split (top half becomes purple)
3. Keep the bottom half as... whatever it was
But the bottom half keeps its original color. So if the original block was white, splitting with node 12=purple gives: purple (top), white (bottom).
Then if I want the bottom to be purple too, I need to merge (which takes node 12's color=purple). But merging requires node 12 ≠ node 13, and after the split, node 13=purple=node 12, so SPACE doesn't work.
Hmm, what if the goal is actually to have THREE blocks: white (node 12), purple (node 13), white (node 14)? That would be:
- White on top (node 12)
- Purple in middle (node 13)
- White on bottom (node 14)
But the panel only shows two blocks. And the level wasn't done when I had this configuration earlier.
Wait, let me re-examine. Maybe the goal is to have the bottom area (nodes 13+14) match the panel. The panel has white on top, purple on bottom. So I need:
- Node 13 = white (50px)
- Node 14 = purple (50px)
To achieve this:
1. Start with node 12=white, node 13=white (100px) [current state]
2. SPACE → splits: node 13=white (50px), node 14=white (50px) [both white since original was white]
Wait, but node 12=node 13=white, so SPACE does nothing!
I need node 12 ≠ node 13 to use SPACE. So:
1. Make node 12=purple
2. SPACE → splits: node 13=purple (50px), node 14=white (50px) [top=purple, bottom=white]
3. Make node 12=white
Result: white, purple, white
But I want white, purple (in the bottom area). The bottom two blocks are purple, white - not white, purple.
Hmm, what if I need to swap the order? The panel has white on top, purple on bottom. The bottom area has purple on top, white on bottom.
What if LEFT/RIGHT or UP/DOWN can swap the order of the bottom blocks?
Let me try LEFT/RIGHT to see what they do.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: node 12=white (84px), node 13=white (100px)
# I need to get the bottom area to have white on top, purple on bottom
# 
# Let me try LEFT/RIGHT to see what they do to the blocks
# First, let me get to a 3-block state to test
predict(expect_change=True, note='Make node 12 purple to enable SPACE')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 43}])

predict(expect_change=True, note='SPACE splits node 13')
action(['SPACE'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After split:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [12, 13, 14]:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Now try LEFT
predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT might swap or rotate bottom blocks')
action(['LEFT'])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter LEFT:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [12, 13, 14]:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
